metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which committed file carries `flip_rate` (either add it to `transition_matrix.json`'s schema and update AC2's jq to assert `has("flip_rate")`, or add it explicitly to `fp_control.json`'s schema in AC5's jq enumeration) so the artifact path in AC4 is unambiguous.
evidence: AC4: `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") and (.flip_rate|type=="number")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/fp_control.json`
AC5 jq enumerates fp_control.json keys as: `new_blocking_median`, `new_advisory_median_delta`, `null_floor_pass_to_block`, `replays`, `r1_pass` — `flip_rate` absent
What section: 'flip_rate = |{PASS→BLOCK}| / |{tickets with baseline_verdict == PASS, included}| — Recorded per candidate' — no file named
AC2 jq on transition_matrix.json checks only cardinality cells, not `flip_rate`
findings.finding: AC4's jq proving command checks `flip_rate` in `fp_control.json`, but the plan's schema for `fp_control.json` (AC5 jq) lists only `new_blocking_median`, `new_advisory_median_delta`, `null_floor_pass_to_block`, `replays`, and `r1_pass` — `flip_rate` is not defined as a key in that file. The full-sample flip rate logically belongs in `transition_matrix.json`, but AC2's cardinality jq for that file does not assert `flip_rate` is present either. An executor implementing the schema has no authoritative file to write `flip_rate` into, and the AC4 jq will silently pass or fail depending on whichever file the implementer chooses.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 proving command (`jq -e 'has("flip_rate")...' .../fp_control.json`) and AC5 proving command (fp_control.json schema)
scenarios: Executor puts flip_rate in transition_matrix.json per logic; AC4 jq targeting fp_control.json fails even though the value is correctly computed.
Executor puts flip_rate in fp_control.json to satisfy AC4; AC5 jq for fp_control.json passes because `has()` checks are OR-independent, but the schema is undocumented and inconsistent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `flip_rate` to `transition_matrix.json` (the natural home for the full-200 aggregate) and update AC2's jq to include `and has("flip_rate")`. Update AC4's proving jq to point to `transition_matrix.json`. Separately, if a per-30-subset flip rate is also desired in `fp_control.json`, add `"fp_subset_flip_rate"` explicitly to the AC5 schema jq.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-flight check in `replay_candidate.py run` (or as a stated manual step) that asserts the active build's rubric/registry identity matches the expected candidate (e.g. hash the loaded rubric file or assert a known candidate-specific constant) and fails loud before any LLM calls are made.
evidence: 'Drive the candidate branch build's gate over the sample via review_plan(tid, ...)' — no preceding step verifies the active build
'Candidate rubric/registry edits live on the candidate's own experiment branch build (per operator direction; no overlay/config flag)' — no AC enforces this
No AC proving command asserts e.g. the candidate's `sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` or rubric hash before running LLM calls
findings.finding: The plan instructs the executor to 'Drive the candidate branch build's gate' via `review_plan`, but provides no step, AC, or proving command that verifies the Python environment is actually running the candidate branch build (rather than the installed default). The only statement is 'per operator direction; no overlay/config flag,' which is operator-attested but has no corresponding AC checkpoint. An executor could silently run the wrong build and produce a meaningless transition matrix.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay' paragraph; Scope — 'No src/rebar behavior change'
scenarios: Executor runs `replay_candidate.py run --candidate r1` while on the main branch; all 200+ billable LLM calls run the current (non-candidate) build; the matrix is committed without detecting the error; the gate decision is invalid.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--candidate-fingerprint <hash>` flag to `replay_candidate.py run` that the executor must supply (derivable from the candidate branch's rubric file); the driver hashes the loaded rubric at startup and asserts equality before issuing any `review_plan` call. Document the derivation command in the README E3 section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the artifact location contradiction for `flip_rate`: either (a) add `flip_rate` explicitly to the What-section description of `fp_control.json`'s schema and clarify it is the 30-plan flip rate on the FP-control subset, or (b) change AC4's jq command to point at `transition_matrix.json` and clarify that flip_rate is derived from the full 200-ticket matrix — whichever matches the intended design.
evidence: AC4 proving: `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") and (.flip_rate|type=="number")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/fp_control.json`
What: 'Primary metric (PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2%). flip_rate = |{PASS→BLOCK}| / |{tickets with baseline_verdict == PASS, included}| — ... Recorded per candidate'
Scope lists fp_control.json as the 30-plan gate result artifact; transition_matrix.json as the matrix artifact
What § 'FP-control gate result': describes fp_control.json as containing 'new_blocking_median', 'new_advisory_median_delta', 'replays', 'null_floor', 'r1_pass' — flip_rate is not listed there
findings.finding: AC4's jq proving step checks for `flip_rate` inside `fp_control.json`, but the plan body describes the PASS→BLOCK flip_rate as a primary metric computed from the full ~200-ticket transition matrix and implies it lives in `transition_matrix.json` (or a per-candidate summary derived from it), not in `fp_control.json` which is explicitly the 30-plan FP-control gate artifact. An implementer following AC4 will put flip_rate in fp_control.json; an implementer following the What section will put it in transition_matrix.json — two incompatible artifacts.
findings.location: AC4 proving jq command (`fp_control.json`) vs What § 'Primary metric (PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2%)'
scenarios: Implementer writes flip_rate into fp_control.json (following AC4) but the analyze subcommand only writes it into transition_matrix.json (following What), so AC4's jq assertion fails at proving time
Implementer writes flip_rate into both files for safety, creating redundant/potentially inconsistent state
Reviewer cannot tell which artifact is authoritative for the ≤2% gate bar
findings.suggested_fix: If flip_rate is meant to be the full-200-ticket metric, change AC4's proving jq to: `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") and (.flip_rate|type=="number")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/transition_matrix.json` and add flip_rate to the Scope's description of transition_matrix.json's contents. If it is also reported in fp_control.json (30-plan sub-rate), state that explicitly in the fp_control.json schema in What.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the driver must compute `sidecar.norm_id(f)` on each candidate finding from `raw_verdicts.jsonl` before the criterion_deltas join, since `review_plan()`'s returned verdict dict does not carry `norm_id` (unlike sidecar payloads).
evidence: Plan: 'Findings join across the two builds on norm_id (sidecar.py:261, reword-tolerant, criterion-scoped)'
Plan: capturing 'full findings[]' from review_plan() to raw_verdicts.jsonl
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:418: 'norm_id': norm_id(f)  — added only in _slim() inside build_payload()
tests/unit/test_sidecar_enrichment.py:69: assert 'norm_id' not in verdict['advisory'][0]  — surfaced verdict findings have no norm_id
The baseline sidecar payload carries norm_id; the candidate raw_verdicts.jsonl does not, unless the driver explicitly computes it.
findings.finding: The plan's criterion_deltas computation states findings are joined across builds on `norm_id`, but `review_plan()` returns a verdict dict whose `blocking[]` and `advisory[]` arrays do NOT carry `norm_id` — it is only added to sidecar payloads (confirmed: `sidecar._slim` adds it at `build_payload` time, and `test_sidecar_enrichment.py` asserts `'norm_id' not in verdict['advisory'][0]`). The driver captures the raw returned verdict dict as `raw_verdicts.jsonl`, so candidate findings lack `norm_id`. The plan does not specify that the driver must compute `sidecar.norm_id(f)` on each candidate finding to enable the join.
findings.location: What section, 'Per-criterion finding delta schema' paragraph; also 'Verdict → matrix mapping' paragraph
scenarios: Implementer writes criterion_deltas assuming candidate findings in raw_verdicts.jsonl carry norm_id, finds the field absent, and the join produces empty new_norm_ids / removed_norm_ids sets — silently computing wrong deltas.
Alternatively, implementer computes norm_id correctly but the plan gave no instruction to do so, making the implementation guesswork.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Per-criterion finding delta schema' section add: 'Because `review_plan()` returns verdict findings without `norm_id` (it is sidecar-only), the driver computes `sidecar.norm_id(f)` on each candidate finding before the join. `e3_metrics.criterion_deltas()` accepts a `norm_id_fn` callable (defaulting to `sidecar.norm_id`) so tests can inject a fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and control for the Pass-1 recall mechanism (`prior_concerns` reads the live baseline sidecar during every candidate replay run): either (a) document the direction and magnitude of the bias (recall is conservative — it biases toward baseline agreement — so the flip-rate estimate is a lower bound), (b) run candidate replays against a sidecar-free temp store per ticket, or (c) confirm a flag exists to disable recall and add it to the replay call signature.
evidence: pass1.py:504: `concerns = sidecar.prior_concerns(ctx.ticket_id, repo_root=ctx.tickets_root)` — called unconditionally, not gated on `sign`, `force`, or `emit_sidecar`
sidecar.py:340: `prior_concerns` calls `latest_review_result()` which reads the on-disk sidecar — the pre-existing baseline sidecar for each sampled ticket
plan says `emit_sidecar=False` prevents mutation, and `force=True` bypasses idempotent reuse — but neither disables recall
ABSENCE: the plan does not mention `prior_concerns`, recall, or any mechanism that injects baseline findings into the candidate run
findings.finding: The candidate replay calls `review_plan(..., sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)`, but the Pass-1 recall mechanism (`prior_concerns` at `pass1.py:504`) is not disabled by any of these flags — it unconditionally reads `latest_review_result()` from the on-disk baseline sidecar and injects up to RECALL_CAP=12 high-priority baseline findings back into the candidate Pass-1 pool. This systematically biases the candidate run toward re-surfacing the same findings as the baseline (inflating PASS→PASS agreement and potentially suppressing genuine PASS→BLOCK flips the candidate rubric would otherwise produce), undermining the counterfactual independence the experiment is designed to measure. The plan does not acknowledge or control for this confound.
findings.location: What → 'The replay' paragraph; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/pass1.py:499-530`; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:328-363`
scenarios: A sampled ticket has a high-priority advisory from its baseline sidecar; `prior_concerns` injects it into the candidate Pass-1 pool; Pass-2 surfaces it in the candidate verdict — the finding appears 'new' by candidate rubric but is actually recalled from the baseline sidecar, artificially reducing the PASS→BLOCK flip rate the experiment is meant to measure.
Conversely, a ticket where the candidate rubric would discover a new blocking finding might instead re-surface a prior advisory-only finding via recall, reducing the measured flip sensitivity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for fingerprint mismatches within the 30-plan FP-control subset: define whether the run should (a) fail loud if any FP-control ticket is mismatched, (b) proceed with the reduced subset and restate the bar as >3/N_included, or (c) replace the mismatched ticket from the PASS-baseline pool before running.
evidence: What §matrix mapping: 'A candidate verdict that is INDETERMINATE … or a material-fingerprint mismatch is excluded from the matrix and every flip-rate denominator'
What §LLM nondeterminism: '30-plan FP-control subset → N=3 candidate replays, metric taken at the median' — no mismatch fallback mentioned
ABSENCE: no specification of whether a fingerprint mismatch within the 30-plan FP-control subset causes the gate to abort, reduce the denominator, or error loud
AC1 asserts `wc -l == 30` on `fp_control_sample.jsonl` but this only verifies the sample file, not the included count at analysis time
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for the case where the 30-plan FP-control subset suffers material fingerprint mismatches during the N=3 candidate replays. The plan specifies that fingerprint-mismatched tickets are excluded from the matrix denominator on the full ~200-sample replay, but the FP-control subset is required to have exactly 30 tickets (AC1 asserts `wc -l == 30`). The plan does not define what the driver should do if one or more of the 30 FP-control tickets has a fingerprint mismatch on the candidate run — whether the R1 gate computation proceeds on the reduced subset (and if so, what the new denominator is), or whether the run aborts.
findings.location: What §'LLM nondeterminism control'; What §'Verdict → matrix mapping'; AC1 (`test $(wc -l < .../fp_control_sample.jsonl) -eq 30`)
scenarios: A candidate changes how `assemble_context` resolves children or description, causing some FP-control tickets to produce different material fingerprints; the driver silently computes `new_blocking_median` on 27 tickets instead of 30, and the gate passes a weaker bar than intended.
An implementer makes the driver abort on any fingerprint mismatch in the FP-control subset, causing the gate to be unrunnable for candidates that legitimately restructure context assembly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `flip_rate` artifact location contradiction: either (a) add `flip_rate` to the AC5 jq schema assertion for `fp_control.json` and clarify it carries the full-sample (not 30-plan) denominator, or (b) move the AC4 proving command to target `transition_matrix.json` and add `flip_rate` to that file's schema in AC2.
evidence: AC4: `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") and (.flip_rate|type=="number")' .../fp_control.json`
AC5 jq: `has('new_blocking_median') and has('new_advisory_median_delta') and has('null_floor_pass_to_block') and (.replays|length==3) and has('r1_pass')` — no `flip_rate` key
What §Primary metric: 'flip_rate = |{PASS→BLOCK}| / |{tickets with baseline_verdict == PASS, included}| — Recorded per candidate'
AC2 jq on transition_matrix.json checks only matrix cardinality cells + excluded.total; no `flip_rate` field asserted

Scope per-candidate file list: raw_verdicts.jsonl, transition_matrix.json, criterion_deltas.jsonl, criterion_deltas_summary.json, fp_control.json — no file named as authoritative for the full-sample flip_rate metric
findings.finding: `fp_control.json` has two incompatible schema definitions: AC4's jq asserts `has('flip_rate')` in that file, but AC5's jq enumerates its keys as {new_blocking_median, new_advisory_median_delta, null_floor_pass_to_block, replays, r1_pass} — `flip_rate` absent. The What section (§'Primary metric') says flip_rate is the full-~200-sample metric and is 'Recorded per candidate'; the Scope lists `transition_matrix.json` as the matrix artifact but no schema for it asserts a `flip_rate` field. An executor implementing `fp_control_eval` and the `analyze` subcommand cannot determine which file to write `flip_rate` into — or whether to write it in both.
findings.location: AC4 proving jq (`fp_control.json`); AC5 jq schema enumeration of `fp_control.json`; What §'Primary metric (PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2%)'
scenarios: Implementer writes flip_rate to transition_matrix.json (natural home for the full-sample matrix metric); AC4's jq on fp_control.json fails even though the value is correctly computed.

Implementer writes flip_rate to fp_control.json (following AC4); the 30-plan denominator is used rather than the full ~200-sample denominator, silently reporting an underpowered estimate as the primary metric.
Implementer writes flip_rate to both files, creating redundancy and potential inconsistency at the gate decision point.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `flip_rate` (full-~200-sample denominator) to `transition_matrix.json` and update AC2's jq to assert `has('flip_rate')` there; change AC4's proving command to target `transition_matrix.json` for the `flip_rate` check. Keep `fp_control.json` for the 30-plan FP-control gate fields only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full `excluded` block schema in the `transition_matrix.json` section of What (e.g. `{total, fingerprint_mismatch, indeterminate}`) and add the sub-field assertions to AC2's jq cardinality check.
evidence: What: 'its count is reported in a separate `excluded` block' — singular 'count', but two distinct exclusion reasons (INDETERMINATE candidate verdict; material fingerprint mismatch) exist
AC2 jq: `(.excluded.total)` — only `.total` sub-field referenced
ABSENCE: no schema definition enumerates the full set of keys within the `excluded` block; an implementer cannot determine whether to break out exclusion subcategories or emit only a total
findings.finding: The `excluded` block in `transition_matrix.json` has an underdefined schema. The plan says fingerprint mismatches and INDETERMINATE candidate verdicts are 'excluded from the matrix and every flip-rate denominator, and its count is reported in a separate `excluded` block.' AC2's cardinality jq asserts `excluded.total` exists, but the field vocabulary of the `excluded` block beyond `.total` is never enumerated — whether it should carry `{total, fingerprint_mismatch_count, indeterminate_count}` or just `{total}` is unspecified.
findings.location: What §'Verdict → matrix mapping'; AC2 proving jq on `transition_matrix.json`
scenarios: Implementer emits only `excluded.total`; a downstream consumer (e.g. README report author) cannot distinguish whether a high exclusion count is from fingerprint drift or INDETERMINATE degradation — losing attributability the plan claims.
Implementer emits `excluded.fingerprint_mismatch` and `excluded.indeterminate` sub-fields; AC2's jq passes but the schema is still unofficial and varies across candidate runs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What §'Verdict → matrix mapping': 'The `excluded` block carries `{total, fingerprint_mismatch, indeterminate}` — the sum of which equals `total`.' Update AC2's jq to also assert `.excluded.fingerprint_mismatch` and `.excluded.indeterminate` are present as numbers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `force=True` rationale in the 'The replay' section to reflect that `sign=False` already suppresses both the idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh short-circuits (per `__init__.py` lines 508, 517), and clarify that `force=True` is a belt-and-suspenders guard for future signed-replay variants.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py line 508: `if sign and not force:` — idempotent reuse is gated on `sign=True`, not just `force=False`
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py line 517: `if sign:` — drift_refresh is also gated on sign
Plan asserts: 'force=True (the default force=False lets _run_plan_review's idempotent-reuse short-circuit return the prior verdict)'
findings.finding: The plan's stated rationale for `force=True` is inaccurate: it claims `force=False` would let `_run_plan_review`'s idempotent-reuse short-circuit fire, but that short-circuit is already suppressed by `sign=False` (the code checks `if sign and not force:` — line 508). Symmetrically, `drift_refresh` is guarded by `if sign:` (line 517), so it too is already suppressed. `force=True` is redundant in the `sign=False` context. The inaccuracy matters because a future caller using `sign=True` (e.g. a signed audit replay) could mistakenly omit `force=True`, silently getting reused verdicts instead of fresh candidate runs.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay' paragraph: 'force=True (the default force=False lets _run_plan_review's idempotent-reuse short-circuit return the *prior* verdict instead of actually re-running the candidate gate)'
scenarios: A future signed replay variant omits force=True because the reviewer reads the plan's rationale, believes sign=False already handles it (correctly), but then switches to sign=True without adding force=True — silently getting stale reused verdicts.
A driver that validates flag combinations against the plan's documented rationale rejects a valid sign=False/force=False call as 'missing the bypass', introducing unnecessary complexity.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'force=True (the default force=False lets _run_plan_review's idempotent-reuse short-circuit return the prior verdict instead of actually re-running the candidate gate)' with 'force=True (belt-and-suspenders: with sign=False the idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh short-circuits are already suppressed by the sign guard in _run_plan_review lines 508/517, but force=True makes the bypass explicit and keeps the driver correct if a future variant uses sign=True).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact key schema of the `excluded` block in `transition_matrix.json` (e.g. `{"fingerprint_mismatch": int, "indeterminate": int, "total": int}`) in the What section, and confirm the AC2 jq path `.excluded.total` matches that schema.
evidence: AC2 jq: `(.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total`
What section: 'is excluded from the matrix and every flip-rate denominator, and its count is reported in a separate `excluded` block' — no key names given
DEFECT TAXONOMY #6: assumed structured path `.excluded.total` without schema definition
findings.finding: AC2's cardinality jq accesses `.excluded.total` on `transition_matrix.json`, but the plan's schema for the `excluded` block in the transition matrix is never defined — the What section only says 'its count is reported in a separate `excluded` block' without naming any keys. If the implementation uses a different key (e.g. `excluded_count`, `n`, or a flat integer), the jq path `.excluded.total` evaluates to `null`, making the arithmetic silently wrong (null + numbers = numbers in jq, so the assertion may produce a false positive).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 proving command; What section — Verdict → matrix mapping paragraph
scenarios: Implementation writes `{"excluded": {"fingerprint_mismatch": 4, "indeterminate": 2}}` with no `total` key; jq evaluates `.excluded.total` as null; arithmetic becomes `PASS+BLOCK cells + null == observed_total` which jq treats as false, causing a spurious failure — or the implementation adds a spurious `total` key to pass the check without actually computing it correctly.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section's matrix description, add a sentence such as: 'The `excluded` block has keys `fingerprint_mismatch` (int), `indeterminate` (int), and `total` (int, their sum).' This makes `.excluded.total` in the AC2 jq unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate a precise, step-by-step computation rule for `new_advisory_median_delta` adjacent to the `fp_control.json` schema definition: specify (1) per-ticket new-advisory count = count of `norm_id`s surfaced as advisory in candidate but absent from frozen baseline surfaced findings for that ticket, (2) this is computed per replay run, (3) the median is taken across the 30 FP-control tickets within each of N=3 replays, then (4) the final value is the median of those 3 per-replay medians.
evidence: What §R1 gate: '(b) the per-review new-advisory-count delta has median >1' — 'New' = norm_id absent from frozen baseline surfaced findings
AC5 jq: `has('new_advisory_median_delta')` — single field name, no further schema definition

What §R1 gate condition (a) uses 'count of subset plans gaining ≥1 new blocking finding' (per-plan binary → median across 30); condition (b) uses 'per-review new-advisory-count delta' (per-plan scalar → median across 30, then median across N=3 replays) — the two aggregation levels are structurally different but never formalized side by side

ABSENCE: no section defines `new_advisory_median_delta` as (median over 30 tickets of (candidate_advisory_norm_id_new_count − 0)) or (median of per-ticket raw count delta); both readings are consistent with the English prose
findings.finding: The `new_advisory_median_delta` computation rule is not co-located with the `fp_control.json` output schema that `fp_control_eval` must produce. The What section states two R1 gate conditions — (a) count of 30-subset plans gaining ≥1 new blocking finding, and (b) 'per-review new-advisory-count delta has median >1'. The schema names the output field `new_advisory_median_delta`, but the precise computation — specifically, whether the per-review delta is `(candidate_surfaced_advisory_count − baseline_surfaced_advisory_count)` per ticket with both counts drawn from the same `norm_id`-new logic, whether that delta is first median'd within each of N=3 replays and then median'd across replays, and what 'new' means here (norm_id-new vs. raw count delta) — is never formally defined adjacent to the fp_control.json schema definition. The two R1 conditions use different aggregation structures that are easy to conflate without a co-located spec.
findings.location: What §'Primary R1 gate (SOTA §5/R1, higher-rigor)'; AC5 jq (`new_advisory_median_delta`); `fp_control_eval` in Scope
scenarios: Implementer computes `new_advisory_median_delta` as the median over the 30-plan subset of the raw advisory-count difference (candidate total advisory − baseline total advisory), missing the `norm_id`-new constraint; the metric passes for noise-reduced advisories but misses genuine new-advisory inflation.
Implementer applies norm_id-new logic correctly for the blocking metric but uses a different counting basis for the advisory metric, making the two R1 conditions inconsistent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the `prior_concerns` recall-injection confound: either (a) document that `emit_sidecar=False` prevents new sidecars from being written during replay so the recall set is stable across replays on the same day, or (b) specify that the driver passes a snapshot of the frozen baseline's `surfaced_findings` as the recall set (overriding the live sidecar read), or (c) add a note that the null-floor replay uses the same live sidecar state as the candidate runs, so the confound cancels in the delta.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/pass1.py line 504: `concerns = sidecar.prior_concerns(ctx.ticket_id, repo_root=ctx.tickets_root)` — called unconditionally during every Pass-1 run
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py lines 328-363: `prior_concerns` reads `latest_review_result` (live sidecar, not frozen baseline)

Plan definition 3: 'step 0 of the driver freezes the baseline for every sampled ticket into runs/e3/baseline.jsonl' — but this freeze applies only to the verdict comparison, not to the recall-injection sidecar
Plan attribution claim: 'a verdict flip is attributable to the candidate change + LLM sampling noise, never to text drift' — prior-concerns injection is a third uncontrolled variable
findings.finding: The plan does not address the `prior_concerns` recall-injection path: every candidate `review_plan` call invokes `sidecar.prior_concerns(ticket_id, ...)` inside `run_pass1`, which reads the **live** (non-frozen) latest sidecar for each sampled ticket. If the production sidecar for any sampled ticket is updated between the baseline-freeze step (Step 0) and the candidate run, the candidate's Pass-1 finder receives a different prior-concerns recall set than the baseline round did. This contaminates the finding delta: a verdict flip could be caused by the changed recall injection, not by the candidate rubric change — violating the plan's core attribution claim.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay' paragraph and Definition 3 (Frozen baseline artifact); also 'LLM nondeterminism control' section
scenarios: A sampled ticket gets a new REVIEW_RESULT sidecar (from an unrelated production review) between baseline-freeze and the candidate run; its updated prior_concerns injects additional findings into the candidate's Pass-1, producing new surfaced findings that are counted as 'new' findings caused by the candidate rubric change.

The null-floor replay (current build over the same 30 plans) also suffers this, partially masking the contamination — but the magnitude differs between the null run and the N=3 candidate runs if the timing differs.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the batch driver wraps each per-ticket `review_plan` call in a try/except, catches hard exceptions (network errors, API 5xx, unexpected raises), logs them as `excluded` rows with `reason: exception` in the checkpoint, and continues to the next ticket — so `assert len(raw_verdicts) + len(excluded_rows) == len(sample)` always holds and the run is resumable.
evidence: 'INDETERMINATE (LLM outage / mid-run degradation — §5.1 shows ~8% of reviews land INDETERMINATE) ... is excluded from the matrix' — only covers the case where review_plan returns a verdict dict with INDETERMINATE, not where it raises
'checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing' — describes checkpointing but not exception catch-and-continue with per-ticket error logging
'assert len(raw_verdicts) == len(sample) before any summary is written' — this assertion will fail if exceptions caused rows to be skipped silently, blocking analysis without a clear remediation path
ABSENCE: no mention of try/except around per-ticket review_plan call, no retry/backoff for transient API errors, no specification that exceptions are caught and logged as excluded rows
findings.finding: The plan specifies handling for INDETERMINATE verdicts (soft LLM failures that return a verdict dict), but does not specify how the batch driver handles hard exceptions thrown by review_plan (network timeouts, API 5xx, unhandled internal errors). If an exception propagates, the driver would abort the in-progress run without logging the failure as an excluded row, violating the completeness assertion and leaving partial raw_verdicts.jsonl in an ambiguous state.
findings.location: What > The replay (batch driver behavior); Acceptance Criteria AC-2 (cardinality assertion)
scenarios: A transient network timeout mid-batch aborts the run after 150 tickets; resume picks up from checkpoint but the 50 failed tickets are neither in raw_verdicts.jsonl nor in an excluded block; the final cardinality assertion fails with no actionable diagnosis
An API rate-limit exception (not an INDETERMINATE verdict) on ticket 87 propagates uncaught, corrupting the checkpoint state
findings.suggested_fix: In replay_candidate.py, wrap each ticket's review_plan call: `try: result = review_plan(...); append_checkpoint(result) except Exception as e: append_excluded(ticket_id, reason=f'exception:{type(e).__name__}', detail=str(e))`. Update the completeness assertion to `assert len(raw_verdicts) + len(excluded_exception_rows) == len(sample)` and report exception-excluded counts alongside INDETERMINATE-excluded counts in the summary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State whether the batch driver inherits rate-limit/backoff behavior from the existing harness or explicitly adds it (e.g., exponential backoff on 429/503 responses, or a configurable inter-call delay), and specify the expected throughput so operators can plan run time for the ~380-call total.
evidence: 'The existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket' — only checkpoint behavior is cited as inherited from precedent, not rate-limit handling
'Total billable candidate runs ≈ 200 (matrix) + 3×30 (FP replication) + 30 (null floor)' — cost is stated but throughput/rate-limit behavior is absent
ABSENCE: no mention of exponential backoff, token-bucket pacing, or provider rate-limit headers in the driver specification
findings.finding: The plan does not specify rate-limit handling for the batch LLM calls (~380 total calls across matrix + FP replication + null floor runs). The existing batch driver precedent is cited only for checkpoint behavior, not for rate-limit/backoff behavior. If the provider enforces per-minute rate limits, rapid sequential calls could trigger 429 errors that are silently converted to exceptions (see T5b finding) or INDETERMINATE verdicts, inflating the excluded count and potentially invalidating the N=3 replication.
findings.location: What > The replay (batch driver behavior); LLM nondeterminism control section
scenarios: Running the 3×30 FP replication back-to-back triggers provider rate limits; multiple tickets land INDETERMINATE due to 429s; the N=3 median is computed over a degraded sample, invalidating the gate decision
The 200-ticket matrix run at full speed exhausts per-minute quota; operator must manually restart in chunks with no guidance

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `e3_metrics.py` is intended to be consumed by >1 caller (e.g. a future EN replay harness); if it will remain a single-consumer module, consider inlining the four pure functions into `replay_candidate.py` or a `_e3_metrics.py` private submodule to avoid premature abstraction (Rule-of-Three: currently 1 named call-site).
evidence: Scope section names exactly one driver consumer: 'replay_candidate.py'
No existing e3_metrics import found anywhere in codebase (search returned no hits)
Existing experiment harnesses (analyze.py, analyze2.py, gate_lib.py, harness.py) do not split pure metric functions into separate modules — they inline them
The testability goal (pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py) is the only stated justification for the split
findings.finding: The plan introduces `e3_metrics.py` as a standalone pure-function module exporting `build_matrix`, `criterion_deltas`, `pass_to_block_flip_rate`, and `fp_control_eval`, but only one consumer is named in the plan (`replay_candidate.py`). The Rule-of-Three threshold (≥3 call-sites) is not met. The sole testability argument (`test_e3_metrics.py`) could be equally satisfied by testing the functions directly within `replay_candidate.py` or as inner helpers. Premature abstraction risk: if `e3_metrics.py` is never imported by a second consumer, the separation adds indirection with no structural benefit.
findings.location: Scope section: '`e3_metrics.py` (pure, LLM-free functions: `build_matrix`, `criterion_deltas`, `pass_to_block_flip_rate`, `fp_control_eval`)'
scenarios: E3 is the only experiment that ever calls these functions; the module becomes a one-off with no reuse payoff.
A future maintainer imports replay_candidate.py and must trace across two files instead of one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify or replace the `run_review_*.py` atomic-append precedent claim: the existing harnesses overwrite the full output file on each tick (not atomic-append); either cite a file that actually demonstrates the intended pattern or specify the checkpoint implementation explicitly in the plan.
evidence: docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_review_children.py line 55: `json.dump(out, open(OUT, 'w'), indent=1)  # checkpoint after each child` — full overwrite, not atomic append
No `atomic append` or `os.replace` pattern found anywhere in harnesses/run_review_*.py files
Plan asserts this as the established 'precedent' guiding how replay_candidate.py should checkpoint
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the existing `harnesses/run_review_*.py` batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing' — but the actual harnesses do not use atomic append. `run_review_children.py` (representative) uses `json.dump(out, open(OUT, 'w'), ...)` which overwrites the full output file on each iteration; there is no atomic-append checkpoint pattern in the existing harnesses. The E3 driver design is instructed to follow this 'precedent,' but the precedent does not demonstrate atomic append, leaving the E3 checkpoint mechanism unbacked.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay' paragraph: 'The existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing'
scenarios: Author implements E3 driver following the stated precedent and discovers the existing harnesses overwrite, not atomically append — the checkpoint/resume design must be invented from scratch, not copied from precedent.
If the driver copies the overwrite pattern from the existing harnesses, a mid-run crash could corrupt or truncate `raw_verdicts.jsonl` rather than allowing safe resume.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'The existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent' with an explicit spec: the driver opens `raw_verdicts.jsonl` in append mode and writes one JSON line per ticket atomically (e.g., `with open(path, 'a') as f: f.write(json.dumps(row) + '\n')` after each `review_plan` call), then resumes by reading already-written ticket_ids on startup and skipping them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the existing `run_review_*.py` harnesses use full-dict overwrite (not atomic JSONL append), and that `replay_candidate.py` must implement true per-ticket atomic append (`.tmp` + `os.replace`) from scratch — not by copying the existing harness pattern.
evidence: Plan: 'The existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing'
docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_review_children.py:55: json.dump(out, open(OUT, 'w'), indent=1)  # checkpoint after each child
This is a full dict overwrite, not an atomic JSONL append; the existing harnesses are not a faithful template for the per-ticket atomic-append behavior the plan specifies.
findings.finding: The plan invokes the existing `harnesses/run_review_*.py` batch drivers as the precedent for 'atomic append' per-ticket checkpointing. Inspection of `run_review_children.py` shows it uses `json.dump(out, open(OUT, 'w'), indent=1)` — a full overwrite of a dict after each ticket, not an atomic append to a JSONL file. The precedent cited does not implement the pattern the plan describes.
findings.location: What section, paragraph beginning 'The existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent'
scenarios: Developer implementing replay_candidate.py follows the existing harnesses and uses json.dump(full_dict, open(...,'w')) instead of atomic per-line append, losing resume-on-interrupt behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the sentence with: 'The driver appends each verdict atomically to `raw_verdicts.jsonl` (write to `raw_verdicts.jsonl.tmp`, then `os.replace`) after every ticket, so a mid-run interrupt resumes from the last committed line. (Note: existing `run_review_*.py` harnesses use full-dict overwrite — do not copy that pattern.)'

findings.finding: The plan calls `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` as the candidate entrypoint but does not specify `sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True`. At default settings (`sign=True, force=False`), `_idempotent_reuse` will short-circuit for every sampled ticket whose existing attestation is still valid — which is the common case for cleanly-closed tickets with unchanged plan material (the exact cohort the plan targets). Those tickets return the baseline verdict verbatim with zero LLM calls, producing a spuriously low flip rate and defeating the candidate replay entirely.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph: 'Drive the candidate branch build's gate over the sample via `rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)`'
findings.suggested_fix: In `replay_candidate.py`, call `review_plan(tid, repo_root=repo_root, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)`. `sign=False` disables both idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh (see `_run_plan_review` guards on `if sign`); `force=True` is an additional defense-in-depth; `emit_sidecar=False` prevents writing candidate findings into the shared sidecar store.

findings.finding: The plan calls `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` as the candidate replay entrypoint but omits the required `sign=False` (or `force=True`) parameter. At default `sign=True, force=False`, `_run_plan_review` invokes `_idempotent_reuse` before running any LLM pass; for cleanly-closed tickets with unchanged plan material (the exact cohort the plan targets), `_idempotent_reuse` returns the prior PASS verdict with zero LLM calls, silently defeating the candidate replay.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph: 'Drive the candidate branch build's gate over the sample via `rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)`'
findings.suggested_fix: Call `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)` in the driver. `force=True` bypasses both `_idempotent_reuse` (line 508) and `drift_refresh` (line 517), ensuring a full LLM pass runs. `sign=False` and `emit_sidecar=False` prevent the experiment from mutating shared state (which the plan already intends — 'do not sign').

findings.finding: The plan calls `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` for the candidate replay and says 'do not sign,' but never translates this into the explicit parameters `sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True` in the driver design. With the default `sign=True, force=False`, `_run_plan_review` will hit `_idempotent_reuse` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:508-511) for every cleanly-closed ticket whose attestation is still valid — the exact cohort the plan targets — and return the prior PASS verdict with zero LLM calls, bypassing the candidate rubric change entirely. The replay would silently produce baseline-identical verdicts, making the flip-rate and FP-control metrics meaningless.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph: 'Drive the candidate branch build's gate over the sample via `rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)`' and 'Run with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and do not sign'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'The replay' paragraph, replace the bare `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` call spec with `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)`. Document the rationale: `force=True` bypasses `_idempotent_reuse` and `drift_refresh`; `sign=False` prevents attestation writes; `emit_sidecar=False` prevents sidecar mutation. These three flags together ensure every sampled ticket runs the full candidate pipeline.

findings.finding: The replay driver is instructed to call `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and 'do not sign', but the plan never instructs passing `emit_sidecar=False`. The `review_plan` signature defaults to `emit_sidecar=True`, so each candidate run will write a REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event into the local store for every one of the ~200 historical tickets. This contradicts the plan's own claim that 'the experiment mutates no shared state': it would overwrite the persisted sidecars for replayed tickets, potentially corrupting a subsequent `all_review_results(tid)` call used to re-freeze the baseline or used in production remediation-mode reads for those tickets.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay' paragraph; also review_plan() signature at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:387-399
findings.suggested_fix: In replay_candidate.py's run subcommand: `review_plan(tid, repo_root=repo_root, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False)`. Document this in the Scope section alongside the existing `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` instruction.

findings.finding: The plan states the experiment 'mutates no shared state' via `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and 'do not sign', but does not suppress `emit_sidecar`. With the default `emit_sidecar=True`, every candidate-run review writes a new `REVIEW_RESULT` event file to the executor's local ticket store for each sampled ticket. This overwrites `latest_review_result` and `prior_concerns` for those tickets on that workspace, so any subsequent production reviews of the same tickets (post-experiment) would reason against candidate-branch findings, not the current-build findings.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph: 'Run with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and do not sign, so the experiment mutates no shared state.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing' — but reading run_review_children.py and run_review_configchildren.py shows these scripts accumulate results in a dict and write once at the end (or per-subprocess); they implement no per-ticket atomic-append checkpointing and no mid-run resume logic. The cited precedent does not demonstrate the pattern the plan relies on.
findings.location: What section — The replay paragraph: 'The existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append)' to 'following the atomic-write pattern (`.tmp` + `os.replace`) used by mine_outcome_corpus.py, checkpoint each ticket result to raw_verdicts.jsonl via atomic append so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing.' This grounds the checkpoint design on an actual in-repo example.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and mitigate the `norm_id` cross-build stability assumption: document in `runs/README.md` whether `norm_id` generation is invariant to rubric edits (criterion renaming, rewordings, new criteria), and add a driver assertion or preflight check that verifies the set of `norm_id` prefixes (criterion codes) is stable between baseline and candidate before computing `criterion_deltas`.
evidence: 'Findings join across the two builds on norm_id (the exact-text id differs across builds by construction — sidecar.py:247-257)'
Plan states R1 'edits the Pass-1 finder rubric' but does not acknowledge that this rubric edit could alter norm_id generation (sidecar.py:261 is 'reword-tolerant, criterion-scoped')
No assertion or guard in the driver is specified to verify norm_id schema stability between baseline and candidate builds
ABSENCE: no documentation of norm_id stability contract or its scope of tolerance for rubric-level changes
findings.finding: The criterion_deltas computation joins baseline and candidate findings on `norm_id` (sidecar.py:261), but the plan does not acknowledge or mitigate the risk that the candidate build — which modifies the Pass-1 finder rubric — could alter `norm_id` generation. If rubric edits change criterion identifiers or the normalization logic, `norm_id`s from baseline and candidate would diverge, causing the cross-build join to silently misattribute (or inflate `new_norm_ids`/`removed_norm_ids`) rather than accurately measuring churn. This cross-build coupling is load-bearing and unacknowledged.
findings.location: What → Per-criterion finding delta schema / 'Findings join across the two builds on norm_id (sidecar.py:247-257)'
scenarios: R1 renames or rewords a criterion identifier → norm_id changes → all findings for that criterion appear as 100% new in the candidate, inflating PASS→BLOCK flip rate attribution spuriously
A candidate rubric adds a new criterion with an id that collides with a baseline norm_id after normalization → silent miscount
findings.suggested_fix: Add a preflight step in `replay_candidate.py analyze` that computes the symmetric difference of criterion codes observed in `baseline.jsonl` vs `raw_verdicts.jsonl` and warns (or fails) if new/removed criteria are found — distinguishing rubric-level norm_id drift from genuine new/removed findings. Document in README.md that norm_id is criterion-scoped and stable only within a given rubric schema version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the three gate thresholds (`FP_BLOCKING_THRESHOLD = 3`, `FP_ADVISORY_MEDIAN_THRESHOLD = 1`, `FLIP_RATE_BAR = 0.02`) as named module-level constants in `e3_metrics.py` with inline comments citing `docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md §5/R1` so threshold provenance is traceable and a spec update triggers a deliberate code change.
evidence: 'R1 FAILs if, on 30 sampled historical plans…>3/30 (10%) gain a new blocking finding, or the median new-advisory count rises by >1 per review'
'flip_rate = |{PASS→BLOCK}| / |{tickets with baseline_verdict == PASS, included}|… the epic bar is ≤ 0.02'

Scope: 'e3_metrics.py (pure, LLM-free functions: build_matrix, criterion_deltas, pass_to_block_flip_rate, fp_control_eval)' — no mention of configurable thresholds or named constants referencing their source
findings.finding: The three gate thresholds (>3/30 new-blocking plans, median advisory delta >1, flip rate ≤2%) are prose-defined in the plan and will be encoded as magic numbers in `e3_metrics.py`'s pure functions. If SOTA §5/R1 later revises these bars, the code diverges silently from the spec with no navigable link between threshold value and authoritative source.
findings.location: What → Primary metric / Primary R1 gate / e3_metrics.py
scenarios: SOTA spec is updated to tighten the false-positive bar from >3/30 to >2/30 as more data is gathered — the hardcoded value in e3_metrics.py is not updated, causing R1 to be evaluated against a stale bar
findings.suggested_fix:
At the top of `e3_metrics.py`, add:
```python
# Gate thresholds sourced from docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md §5/R1
FP_BLOCKING_COUNT_BAR = 3        # >3/30 plans gaining new blocking finding → FAIL
FP_ADVISORY_MEDIAN_BAR = 1       # median new-advisory delta >1 → FAIL
PASS_TO_BLOCK_FLIP_RATE_BAR = 0.02  # aggregate flip rate bar
```
Then reference these constants in `fp_control_eval` and `pass_to_block_flip_rate` rather than inline literals.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
